title: The Static Embedding Limit
concept: Static embeddings (Word2Vec) assign one vector per word, failing to handle polysemy (e.g. 'bank' as land vs finance).

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a famous example of a static embedding model?
exercises.explain: Word2Vec is a classic example of a static embedding generator, where each word gets one fixed vector.

cards.title: One Word, Many Meanings
cards.body: How does a computer know the difference between 'river bank' and 'money bank'? For a long time, the answer was: it couldn't! Early AI models got highly confused by words with multiple meanings because they used 'static embeddings'. Let's explore why this happens.

cards.title: What is a Static Embedding?
cards.body: A static embedding is a system (like Word2Vec) that assigns a single, permanent list of numbers (a vector) to each word. It cannot adapt to how the word is used in a sentence, meaning it treats every spelling of a word as having just one fixed meaning.

cards.title: The One-Outfit Wardrobe
cards.body: Imagine giving each person in your city a single outfit they must wear forever. If you give a chef a chef's uniform, they look great at work. But if they go to sleep or go swimming in that same heavy chef's outfit, it doesn't make sense anymore! Static embeddings do the same thing: they force a word like 'bank' to wear one 'outfit' (vector) no matter where it goes.

cards.title: The Apple Confusion
cards.body: Consider the word 'apple'. In the sentence 'I ate a sweet apple,' it refers to a fruit. In 'I bought a new Apple computer,' it refers to a tech company. A static embedding model uses the exact same mathematical vector for 'apple' in both sentences, mixing up the fruit and the company.

cards.title: Myth: AI Knows Context Automatically
cards.body: Many people think that because AI is smart, it automatically understands sentences word-by-word. But static models like Word2Vec look at words in isolation. They do not look around at neighboring words to adjust the meaning of the word they are translating into numbers.

cards.title: Static vs. Dynamic Reality
cards.body: Let's look at how static embeddings see the word 'bank' compared to how humans see it in context.

right.text: A river bank and a financial bank are two completely different concepts.

left.text: One vector represents both 'river bank' and 'money bank' as the exact same point.

exercises.prompt: Why do static embeddings like Word2Vec struggle with words that have multiple meanings?

options.text: They assign only one fixed vector to each word, ignoring context.
They require too much computer memory to run.
They can only understand numbers, not text.
They only work for very long sentences.

exercises.explain: Because static embeddings allocate exactly one vector to each unique word, they cannot adjust when a word has multiple meanings depending on its context.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Static embeddings like Word2Vec can easily tell the difference between 'river bank' and 'savings bank'.
exercises.explain: Static embeddings are limited to one vector per word. Later models, like BERT, introduced contextual embeddings to solve this problem.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about a major limitation of static embeddings.
exercises.sentence: Static embeddings fail to handle [blank], which is when a single word has multiple different meanings.
exercises.explain: Polysemy is the term for words with multiple meanings, which static embeddings struggle to represent correctly because they only allow one vector per word.

exercises.prompt: Two AI agents are predicting how a static Word2Vec model will represent 'light' in 'feather light' vs 'turn on the light'. Which agent is correct?

options.text: AI Agent A: 'The model will dynamically change the vector for light based on the other words.'
AI Agent B: 'The model will use the exact same vector for light in both sentences, failing to show the difference.'
AI Agent C: 'The model will refuse to process the second sentence because it already saw the word light.'

exercises.explain: AI Agent B is correct because static embeddings only assign one fixed vector per word, making it impossible to separate the two meanings of 'light'. AI Agent A mistakenly thinks static embeddings can adapt, and AI Agent C suggests it fails completely.

exercises.prompt: If you feed the sentences 'I run a marathon' and 'The computer run is complete' into a static Word2Vec model, what will happen to the vector representation of the word 'run'?

options.text: They will have completely different vectors.
They will have the exact same vector.
The model will crash trying to process them.
The vector will be split in half.

exercises.explain: Static embeddings like Word2Vec cannot change their output based on context. Therefore, they will assign the exact same vector to 'run' in both sentences, completely missing the change in meaning.
